setting:
A classic closed-circle manor mystery set in rural England in the late 1920s. On a stormy autumn evening, a select group of guests has gathered at an old country estate for a formal masquerade and private supper. By nightfall, the roads are flooded, the grounds are impassable, and the household is sealed off from the outside world until morning. The lead investigator must already be present inside the estate before the isolation begins, having been specifically invited by the eventual victim to witness or advise on a planned private revelation, accusation, or reckoning later that evening.

The story should feel like a traditional fair-play whodunit: atmospheric, elegant, and deduction-centered. It must be historically and technologically consistent with the late 1920s. No modern communications, digital devices, surveillance systems, database searches, or advanced forensic procedures may be used. The mystery must unfold through interviews, conflicting testimony, timing discrepancies, room access, physical traces, written documents, household objects, and careful reasoning. The investigator's presence must be narratively justified as part of the evening's social arrangement, not as someone who conveniently arrives from outside after the murder.

The estate should provide a rich but finite investigative space, including rooms such as a library, study, ballroom, conservatory, dining room, guest wing, servant corridors, and private chambers. The characters should be socially plausible for the period and setting: aristocratic family members, family friends, staff, professional associates, or dependents, each carrying secrets, anxieties, and conflicting interests. Themes may include inheritance, scandal, debt, blackmail, jealousy, class tension, and fear of public disgrace.

The crime should be designed as a layered but fair mystery with one true culprit, one victim, at least four serious suspects, at least eight evidence items, at least one red herring arc, at least two alibi checks, and at least one deliberate attempt to mislead the investigation. The hidden truth must support a long-form investigation with at least fifteen substantial plot beats, culminating in a final confrontation grounded in a clear and coherent chain of evidence.

victim.name: Sir Alistair Thorne
victim.description: A wealthy, abrasive industrialist with a history of predatory business practices and colonial shipping interests.

culprit.name: Eleanor Vance
culprit.description: The doctor's quiet, observant wife and an expert amateur botanist.
culprit.means: Extracted Aconitine (monkshood) from the manor's conservatory.
culprit.motive: To protect her husband from Alistair's blackmail, which was destroying Percival's health and sanity.
culprit.opportunity: Briefly left the masquerade at 9:45 PM while the music was at its loudest.
culprit.alibi: Claims she was in the powder room fixing her mask, but the attendant was away.

suspects.name: Julian Thorne
suspects.description: The victim's estranged son, a high-stakes gambler with mounting debts in London.
suspects.means: Access to a decorative hunting knife kept in the library.
suspects.motive: Accelerating his inheritance to pay off violent debt collectors.
suspects.opportunity: Slipped out of the ballroom for twenty minutes during the masquerade.
suspects.alibi: Claims he was on the terrace clearing his head from the heat, but no one saw him.

suspects.name: Lady Elspeth Thorne
suspects.description: Sir Alistair's second wife, younger and socially ambitious, hiding a secret affair.
suspects.means: Knowledge of the manor's layout and household routines.
suspects.motive: Fear of being disinherited after Alistair threatened to divorce her for her infidelity.
suspects.opportunity: Was unaccounted for during the supper transition to the masquerade.
suspects.alibi: Claims she was in the library reading, though her book remained on the shelf.

suspects.name: Dr. Percival Vance
suspects.description: The family physician who has served the Thornes for twenty years.
suspects.means: Access to lethal sedatives and medical instruments.
suspects.motive: Alistair was blackmailing him over a fatal surgical error he covered up years ago.
suspects.opportunity: Had a scheduled private meeting with Alistair in the study at 10:15 PM.
suspects.alibi: Claims he was in the washroom and then preparing his medical bag for a house call.

suspects.name: Eleanor Vance
suspects.description: The doctor's quiet, observant wife and an expert amateur botanist.
suspects.means: Extracted Aconitine (monkshood) from the manor's conservatory.
suspects.motive: To protect her husband from Alistair's blackmail, which was destroying Percival's health and sanity.
suspects.opportunity: Briefly left the masquerade at 9:45 PM while the music was at its loudest.
suspects.alibi: Claims she was in the powder room fixing her mask, but the attendant was away.

motive: Eleanor discovered Sir Alistair was blackmailing her husband, Dr. Percival Vance, regarding a past medical mistake. She knew Alistair would never stop and that her husband was on the verge of suicide or ruin. She decided to eliminate the threat to save her husband and their social standing.
method: Eleanor poisoned Alistair's silver brandy flask with concentrated Aconitine extracted from monkshood plants in the conservatory. Alistair took a private nightcap in his study as part of his nightly ritual, leading to swift cardiac failure.

true_timeline.summary: The guests arrive at Blackwood Manor as the storm begins to intensify; the roads start flooding.
A tense formal dinner where Alistair hints at a 'grand reckoning' and an upcoming announcement.
Alistair privately pulls Arthur Penhaligon aside, asking him to witness a 'confession of a leech' at midnight.
The masquerade begins; guests are masked and costumes make identification difficult from a distance.
Eleanor Vance slips into the conservatory to retrieve a concentrated monkshood extract she prepared earlier.
Eleanor enters the study and doses the brandy in Alistair's silver flask with the poison.
Alistair retires to his study early and drinks from the poisoned flask, dying almost immediately.
Dr. Percival Vance enters for his scheduled meeting, finds Alistair dead, and panics; he hides a blackmail note he finds on the desk.
Julian Thorne enters the study to steal his IOU notes from the desk; he finds the body, tears his costume ribbon in his haste, and flees.
Julian Thorne stabs the corpse with a library knife to make it look like a struggle, unaware the poison already acted.
Arthur Penhaligon, concerned by the lack of activity, enters the study and discovers the body.

evidence_items.name: Silver Brandy Flask
evidence_items.description: Contains traces of high-quality brandy and concentrated Aconitine.

evidence_items.name: Torn Green Silk Ribbon
evidence_items.description: A ribbon from a masquerade costume, snagged on the desk corner.

evidence_items.name: Stained Suede Glove
evidence_items.description: A woman's gardening glove with traces of crushed plant matter and dirt.

evidence_items.name: Blackmail Letter
evidence_items.description: A note from Alistair to 'P' demanding 5,000 pounds to keep a 'medical secret'.

evidence_items.name: Missing Ledger Page
evidence_items.description: A page detailing Julian's gambling debts, recently torn from the victim's books.

evidence_items.name: Smudged Wine Glass
evidence_items.description: A glass bearing Lady Elspeth's unique shade of lipstick.

evidence_items.name: Dried Monkshood Petal
evidence_items.description: A small purple petal found inside a beaded evening handbag.

evidence_items.name: Decorative Hunting Knife
evidence_items.description: The weapon used for the post-mortem stab wound, wiped clean of prints.

red_herrings.explanation: Julian's torn ribbon and the knife suggest a violent struggle over his debts. In reality, he arrived after the death and committed the theft and the stabbing in a panicked attempt to cover his trail and reclaim his IOU notes.
